sourceRefs.fallbackText: This PEP proposes adding an optional strict boolean keyword parameter to the built-in zip . When enabled, a ValueError is raised if one of the arguments is exhausted before the others.
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip
sourceRefs.textQuote: This PEP proposes adding an optional strict boolean keyword parameter to the built-in zip . When enabled, a ValueError is raised if one of the arguments is exhausted before the others.

highSignalBlocks.text: This PEP proposes adding an optional strict boolean keyword parameter to the built-in zip . When enabled, a ValueError is raised if one of the arguments is exhausted before the others.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It is clear from the author’s personal experience and a survey of the standard library that much (if not most) zip usage involves iterables that must be of equal length. Sometimes this invariant is proven true from the context of the surrounding code, but often the data being zipped is passed from the caller, sourced separately, or generated in some fashion.
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip
sourceRefs.textQuote: It is clear from the author’s personal experience and a survey of the standard library that much (if not most) zip usage involves iterables that must be of equal length. Sometimes this invariant is proven true from the c

highSignalBlocks.text: It is clear from the author’s personal experience and a survey of the standard library that much (if not most) zip usage involves iterables that must be of equal length. Sometimes this invariant is proven true from the context of the surrounding code, but often the data being zipped is passed from the caller, sourced separately, or generated in some fashion. In any of these cases, the default behavior of zip means that faulty refactoring or logic errors could easily result in silently losing data. These bugs are not only difficult to diagnose, but difficult to even detect at all.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It is easy to come up with simple cases where this could be a problem. For example, the following code may work fine when items is a sequence, but silently start producing shortened, mismatched results if items is refactored by the caller to be a consumable iterator:
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip

highSignalBlocks.text: It is easy to come up with simple cases where this could be a problem. For example, the following code may work fine when items is a sequence, but silently start producing shortened, mismatched results if items is refactored by the caller to be a consumable iterator:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: def apply_calculations ( items ): transformed = transform ( items ) for i , t in zip ( items , transformed ): yield calculate ( i , t )
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip
sourceRefs.textQuote: def apply_calculations ( items ): transformed = transform ( items ) for i , t in zip ( items , transformed ): yield calculate ( i , t )

highSignalBlocks.text: def apply_calculations ( items ): transformed = transform ( items ) for i , t in zip ( items , transformed ): yield calculate ( i , t )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There are several other ways in which zip is commonly used. Idiomatic tricks are especially susceptible, because they are often employed by users who lack a complete understanding of how the code works. One example is unpacking into zip to lazily “unzip” or “transpose” nested iterables:
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip
sourceRefs.textQuote: There are several other ways in which zip is commonly used. Idiomatic tricks are especially susceptible, because they are often employed by users who lack a complete understanding of how the code works. One example is un

highSignalBlocks.text: There are several other ways in which zip is commonly used. Idiomatic tricks are especially susceptible, because they are often employed by users who lack a complete understanding of how the code works. One example is unpacking into zip to lazily “unzip” or “transpose” nested iterables:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> x = [[ 1 , 2 , 3 ], [ "one" "two" "three" ]] >>> xt = list ( zip ( * x ))
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In the first case, non-rectangular data is usually a logic error. In the second case, data with a length that is not a multiple of n is often an error as well. However, both of these idioms will silently omit the tail-end items of malformed input.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Perhaps most convincingly, the use of zip in the standard-library ast module created a bug in literal_eval which silently dropped parts of malformed nodes :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When the built-in zip is called with the keyword-only argument strict=True , the resulting iterator will raise a ValueError if the arguments are exhausted at differing lengths. This error will occur at the point when iteration would normally stop today.
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This change is fully backward-compatible. zip currently takes no keyword arguments, and the “non-strict” default behavior when strict is omitted remains unchanged.
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip

sourceRefs.fallbackText: An approximate Python translation is:
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip
sourceRefs.textQuote: An approximate Python translation is:

highSignalBlocks.text: An approximate Python translation is:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: def zip ( * iterables , strict = False ): if not iterables : return iterators = tuple ( iter ( iterable ) for iterable in iterables ) try : while True : items = [] for iterator in iterators : items . append ( next ( iterator )) yield tuple ( items ) except StopIteration : if not strict : return if items : i = len ( items ) plural = " " if i == 1 else "s 1-"
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip
sourceRefs.textQuote: def zip ( * iterables , strict = False ): if not iterables : return iterators = tuple ( iter ( iterable ) for iterable in iterables ) try : while True : items = [] for iterator in iterators : items . append ( next ( iter

highSignalBlocks.text: def zip ( * iterables , strict = False ): if not iterables : return iterators = tuple ( iter ( iterable ) for iterable in iterables ) try : while True : items = [] for iterator in iterators : items . append ( next ( iterator )) yield tuple ( items ) except StopIteration : if not strict : return if items : i = len ( items ) plural = " " if i == 1 else "s 1-" msg = f "zip() argument { i + 1 } is shorter than argument { plural }{ i } " raise ValueError ( msg ) sentinel = object () for i , iterator in enumerate ( iterators [ 1 :], 1 ): if next ( iterator , sentinel ) is not sentinel : plural = " " if i == 1 else "s 1-" msg = f "zip() argument { i + 1 } is longer than argument { plural }{ i } " raise ValueError ( msg )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This is the alternative with the most support on the Python-Ideas mailing list, so it deserves to be discussed in some detail here. It does not have any disqualifying flaws, and could work well enough as a substitute if this PEP is rejected.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: With that in mind, this section aims to outline why adding an optional parameter to zip is a smaller change that ultimately does a better job of solving the problems motivating this PEP.
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It seems that a great deal of the motivation driving this alternative is that zip_longest already exists in itertools . However, zip_longest is in many ways a much more complicated, specialized utility: it takes on the responsibility of filling in missing values, a job neither of the other variants needs to concern themselves with.

However, zip_longest is in many ways a much more complicated, specialized utility: it takes on the responsibility of filling in missing values, a job neither of the other variants needs to concern themselves with.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If both zip and zip_longest lived alongside each other in itertools or as builtins, then adding zip_strict in the same location would indeed be a much stronger argument. However, the new “strict” variant is conceptually much closer to zip in interface and behavior than zip_longest , while still not meeting the high bar of being its own builtin. Given this si
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip
sourceRefs.textQuote: If both zip and zip_longest lived alongside each other in itertools or as builtins, then adding zip_strict in the same location would indeed be a much stronger argument. However, the new “strict” variant is conceptually

highSignalBlocks.text: If both zip and zip_longest lived alongside each other in itertools or as builtins, then adding zip_strict in the same location would indeed be a much stronger argument. However, the new “strict” variant is conceptually much closer to zip in interface and behavior than zip_longest , while still not meeting the high bar of being its own builtin. Given this situation, it seems most natural for zip to grow this new option in-place.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If zip is capable of preventing this class of bug, it becomes much simpler for users to enable the check at call sites with this property. Compare this with importing a drop-in replacement for a built-in utility, which feels somewhat heavy just to check a tricky condition that should “always” be true.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This option only makes more sense than a binary flag if we anticipate having three or more modes. The “obvious” three choices for these enumerated or constant modes would be “shortest” (the current zip behavior), “strict” (the proposed behavior), and “longest” (the itertools.zip_longest behavior).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: However, it doesn’t seem like adding behaviors other than the current default and the proposed “strict” mode is worth the additional complexity. The clearest candidate, “longest”, would require a new fillvalue parameter (which is meaningless for both other modes). This mode is also already handled perfectly by itertools.zip_longest , and adding it would crea
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip
sourceRefs.textQuote: However, it doesn’t seem like adding behaviors other than the current default and the proposed “strict” mode is worth the additional complexity. The clearest candidate, “longest”, would require a new fillvalue parameter

highSignalBlocks.text: However, it doesn’t seem like adding behaviors other than the current default and the proposed “strict” mode is worth the additional complexity. The clearest candidate, “longest”, would require a new fillvalue parameter (which is meaningless for both other modes). This mode is also already handled perfectly by itertools.zip_longest , and adding it would create two ways of doing the same thing. It’s not clear which would be the “obvious” choice: the mode parameter on the built-in zip , or the long-lived namesake utility in itertools .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This proposal is further complicated by the fact that CPython’s actual zip type is currently an undocumented implementation detail. This means that choosing one of the above behaviors will effectively “lock in” the current implementation (or at least require it to be emulated) going forward.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There is nothing “wrong” with the default behavior of zip , since there are many cases where it is indeed the correct way to handle unequally-sized inputs. It’s extremely useful, for example, when dealing with infinite iterators.

sourceRefs.textQuote: itertools.zip_longest already exists to service those cases where the “extra” tail-end data is still needed.

highSignalBlocks.text: itertools.zip_longest already exists to service those cases where the “extra” tail-end data is still needed.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: While able to do basically anything a user could need, this solution makes handling the more common cases (like rejecting mismatched lengths) unnecessarily complicated and non-obvious.

headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip
sourceRefs.textQuote: Raised when an assert statement fails.

highSignalBlocks.text: Raised when an assert statement fails.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Since this feature has nothing to do with Python’s assert statement, raising an AssertionError here would be inappropriate. Users desiring a check that is disabled in optimized mode (like an assert statement) can use strict=__debug__ instead.
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip
sourceRefs.textQuote: Since this feature has nothing to do with Python’s assert statement, raising an AssertionError here would be inappropriate. Users desiring a check that is disabled in optimized mode (like an assert statement) can use str

highSignalBlocks.text: Since this feature has nothing to do with Python’s assert statement, raising an AssertionError here would be inappropriate. Users desiring a check that is disabled in optimized mode (like an assert statement) can use strict=__debug__ instead.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This PEP does not propose any changes to map , since the use of map with multiple iterable arguments is quite rare. However, this PEP’s ruling shall serve as precedent such a future discussion (should it occur).
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip
Add A Similar Feature to map
sourceRefs.textQuote: This PEP does not propose any changes to map , since the use of map with multiple iterable arguments is quite rare. However, this PEP’s ruling shall serve as precedent such a future discussion (should it occur).

highSignalBlocks.text: This PEP does not propose any changes to map , since the use of map with multiple iterable arguments is quite rare. However, this PEP’s ruling shall serve as precedent such a future discussion (should it occur).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If rejected, the feature is realistically not worth pursuing. If accepted, such a change to map should not require its own PEP (though, like all enhancements, its usefulness should be carefully considered). For consistency, it should follow same API and semantics debated here for zip .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This option is perhaps the least attractive.
headingPath: PEP 618 – Add Optional Length-Checking To zip
sourceRefs.textQuote: This option is perhaps the least attractive.

highSignalBlocks.text: This option is perhaps the least attractive.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Silently truncated data is a particularly nasty class of bug, and hand-writing a robust solution that gets this right isn’t trivial . The real-world motivating examples from Python’s own standard library are evidence that it’s very easy to fall into the sort of trap that this feature aims to avoid.
